question_text: Light has a very high frequency of 5 × 10¹⁴ Hz and travels at 3 × 10⁸ m/s in air. Sound has a much lower frequency of 100 Hz and travels at 340 m/s. Calculate the wavelength of each wave and explain why light can be focused with a lens but sound cannot, relating your explanation to wavelength.
correct_answer: Light: 6 × 10⁻⁷ m (600 nm); Sound: 3.4 m. Light has an extremely small wavelength compared to lenses, so it can be focused precisely. Sound has a large wavelength (3.4 m), which is much larger than lens openings, so it diffracts around the lens and cannot be focused effectively.
explanation: Light wavelength: (3 × 10⁸) ÷ (5 × 10¹⁴) = 6 × 10⁻⁷ m. Sound wavelength: 340 ÷ 100 = 3.4 m. Light's tiny wavelength allows it to interact with small lenses without significant diffraction. Sound's large wavelength causes it to diffract around objects, preventing it from being focused by a normal lens. Wavelength determines how much diffraction occurs.
